original_abstract:
This study examines the evolution of Polish–Moldovan relations from 1492 to 1538, a transformative period shaped by shifting dynastic ambitions and burgeoning imperial pressures. The timeline begins with the accession of Jan Olbracht to the Polish throne and concludes with the downfall of Hospodar Petru Rareş. This period ended with the definitive political subordination of Moldova to the Ottoman Empire and the resolution of the long-standing territorial dispute over Pokuttya. A significant geopolitical shift occurred during these years as the Turkish conquest of Budjak established a direct, contiguous border between Poland and the Ottoman state.

The author provides a detailed analysis of how successive Polish monarchs—Jan Olbracht, Alexander Jagiellon, and Sigismund I the Old—navigated their complex relationship with their Moldovan neighbors. Utilizing previously unexamined documents from the Levoča congress, the research sheds new light on the strategic significance of this meeting in the preparations for Jan Olbracht’s 1497 expedition. The study further highlights the influential role of the Chodecki family in fueling border conflicts and delves into the sophisticated diplomatic maneuvering of Petru Rareş as he balanced the competing interests of Poland, Hungary, and Türkiye.

Drawing on an extensive multinational research query across German, Austrian, Romanian, Moldovan, Hungarian, Polish, and Russian archives, the monograph places these bilateral relations within a broad international framework. Since 15th- and 16th-century Moldova served as a critical crossroads for the interests of Poland, Hungary, the Ottoman Empire, and the Grand Duchy of Moscow, the author argues that understanding these ties requires a deep dive into the specific geopolitical and legal landscapes of each involved party. The resulting conclusions offer a fresh perspective on the intricacies of power and diplomacy in Central and Eastern Europe at the dawn of the early modern era.
humanized_abstract:
The monograph presents the development of Polish-Moldovan relations in the years 1492-1538. The period under consideration is full of political intrigues of individual dynasties and growing influence of expanding empires. The beginning date is linked with the accession of the prince Jan Olbracht to the Polish throne, while the end – with the fall of the hospodar Petru Rareş. At that time, Moldova was for good incorporated into the Ottoman Empire, and the conflict over Pokuttya was brought to a conclusion. During this period a substantial political change took place: the Turks conquered Budjak, and Poland for the first time in its history gained a direct border with the Ottoman Empire.

Monarchy, foreign policy and the problems of the Polish–Moldavian border in the reigns of John Albert, Alexander and Sigismund I Old The author presents in detail the monarchs’ attitudes towards the problem of the Moldovan neighbour, which was very important for the period of their reigns. They drew upon their own as well as previous experience. The author uses the source material that has not been taken into account in historical literature yet, such as the Levoča congress documents, which have allowed the author to contribute to the discussion on the importance of the Levoča congress in terms of preparation for John Albert’s campaign of 1497. The author also presents the role of Chodecki’s family, who triggered the border skirmishes, and examines the complex system of diplomacy which Petru Rareş organised to protect Moldova’s interests in relation to the Polish, Hungarian and the Ottoman-Turkish diplomacy.

This study reconstructs the complex network of bilateral relations between early modern Moldova and its major trading partners through the results of a multinational archival research project targeting German, Austrian, Romanian, Moldovan, Hungarian, Polish, and Russian sources. Drawing on state-of-the-art methodologies in international history, the study sets these diplomatic relations in an expansive international context while also addressing local conditions such as the position of a region like medieval and early modern Moldova at the crossroads of Polish, Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian expansion since the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. A thorough investigation of these specific political and institutional contexts allows for a unique analysis of the exercise of power and diplomacy in early modern Central and Eastern Europe, a region that has not yet been fully mapped in the era of modern state formation.
